critic_raw:
The design explicitly acknowledges the temporal nature of 5-year EHR data and admits the stratified random split is 'unconventional given the data's temporal nature,' yet proceeds with it under a 'weak temporal autocorrelation' assumption. For hospital readmission data, this assumption is demonstrably implausible: discharge patterns, readmission rates, and treatment protocols shift over 5 years due to policy changes, new clinical guidelines, and seasonal illness patterns. A random split mixes observations from 2018 (pre-policy) with 2022 (post-policy) in both train and test, allowing the model to achieve good AUROC not by learning patient risk factors but by learning time-period signatures, then being tested on the same time periods.

The design mentions 'policy changes and socio-economic shifts' are 'partially controlled by incorporating them as additional covariates.' But including time-period covariates in a randomly-split dataset does not reproduce deployment conditions — in deployment the model is trained on past patients and applied to future patients. Adding policy indicators as features makes the model retrospectively better at explaining past data but does not validate it for prospective use.

The 'time-series analysis' model is described as an RNN capturing 'temporal dependencies in treatment sequences,' but 30-day readmission prediction is a patient-level outcome, not a sequence forecasting task. Each patient produces one label. The RNN processes the treatment timeline within a single admission and produces a single prediction. The temporal structure being modeled is intra-patient sequence (within a single hospitalization), which is fundamentally different from inter-patient temporal trends. The design conflates these two levels of temporal analysis.

No patient deduplication strategy is mentioned for patients with multiple admissions over 5 years. Multiple admissions per patient means that the same patient appears multiple times in the dataset with different feature snapshots. Random splitting without patient-level holdout creates direct patient-level leakage: the model sees patient X's first admission in training and predicts their second admission in the test set, exploiting stable patient-level covariates.

AUROC is the primary metric, which is appropriate, but the class imbalance (30-day readmission rates are typically 15–20% for heart failure) means AUROC is relatively insensitive to improvements in the minority class precision. Calibration (Brier score, calibration curves) is not mentioned, which is clinically critical for ranking patients for intervention — a well-ranked but poorly calibrated model produces unreliable risk estimates.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random splitting of 5-year EHR data allows the model to learn time-period signatures (treatment protocols, policy environments) that are present in both train and test sets, conflating retrospective explanatory power with prospective predictive validity.

Including policy-period covariates as features does not replicate deployment conditions — in production the model is always trained on historical patients and applied to future patients, a regime the random split does not test.

The RNN models intra-patient treatment sequence within a single admission, not inter-patient temporal trends; the design conflates two distinct levels of temporal structure and does not justify that within-admission sequencing adds predictive value beyond admission-level summary features.

Multiple admissions per patient across 5 years create patient-level leakage when using random splitting — the model can observe an earlier admission of the same patient during training and exploit stable patient characteristics to predict later admissions in the test set.

Calibration metrics (Brier score, calibration curves) are absent; for clinical risk stratification, a well-ranked but poorly calibrated model produces unreliable intervention prioritization, and AUROC alone does not capture this.
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random splitting of 5-year EHR data allows the model to learn time-period signatures (treatment protocols, policy environments) that are present in both train and test sets, conflating retrospective explanatory power with prospective predictive validity.

Including policy-period covariates as features does not replicate deployment conditions — in production the model is always trained on historical patients and applied to future patients, a regime the random split does not test.

The RNN models intra-patient treatment sequence within a single admission, not inter-patient temporal trends; the design conflates two distinct levels of temporal structure and does not justify that within-admission sequencing adds predictive value beyond admission-level summary features.

Multiple admissions per patient across 5 years create patient-level leakage when using random splitting — the model can observe an earlier admission of the same patient during training and exploit stable patient characteristics to predict later admissions in the test set.

Calibration metrics (Brier score, calibration curves) are absent; for clinical risk stratification, a well-ranked but poorly calibrated model produces unreliable intervention prioritization, and AUROC alone does not capture this.
